title: Disentangling anthropogenic stimuli: visual and acoustic components differentially impact captive pied tamarin behaviour
abstract: Anthropogenic activity is increasingly disturbing wildlife, leading to negative impacts on species welfare and conservation. Despite this, very few studies have investigated how the different components of anthropogenic activity (including visual, acoustic, or other components) differentially affect animals. Such studies are important to better understand what animals may perceive as aversive in order to improve, not only captive management and welfare, but also wild management. This study aimed to disentangle the impacts of the visual and acoustic components of two different anthropogenic stimuli (zoo visitors and construction activity) on the critically endangered pied tamarin (Saguinus bicolor). We exposed 12 pair-housed pied tamarins at Jersey Zoo to either no stimuli (baseline), individual components (visual or acoustic stimuli only), or combinations of components (54 trials in total). We found that pied tamarins varied in their responses to the two different stimuli and to their different components, especially when presented in isolation. Visitor visual and visitor acoustic components presented in isolation led to increased vigilance. The combined presentation of visitor components weakened responses, indicating that simultaneous exposure to visual and auditory cues may provide more perceptual context to the primates. In contrast, construction noise caused a significant decrease in environmental engagement (indicated by decreased foraging and increased retreat behaviour). These findings emphasize the importance of understanding how animals respond to different sensory components of stimuli to improve captive management practices, enhance welfare, and ultimately help the conservation of endangered species.

differentially impact captive pied tamarin behaviour

A B S T R A C T
Anthropogenic activity is increasingly disturbing wildlife, leading to negative impacts on species welfare and 
conservation. Despite this, very few studies have investigated how the different components of anthropogenic 
activity (including visual, acoustic, or other components) differentially affect animals. Such studies are important 
to better understand what animals may perceive as aversive in order to improve, not only captive management 
and welfare, but also wild management. This study aimed to disentangle the impacts of the visual and acoustic 
components of two different anthropogenic stimuli (zoo visitors and construction activity) on the critically en­
dangered pied tamarin (Saguinus bicolor). We exposed 12 pair-housed pied tamarins at Jersey Zoo to either no 
stimuli (baseline), individual components (visual or acoustic stimuli only), or combinations of components (54 
trials in total). We found that pied tamarins varied in their responses to the two different stimuli and to their 
different components, especially when presented in isolation. Visitor visual and visitor acoustic components 
presented in isolation led to increased vigilance. The combined presentation of visitor components weakened 
responses, indicating that simultaneous exposure to visual and auditory cues may provide more perceptual 
context to the primates. In contrast, construction noise caused a significant decrease in environmental engage­
ment (indicated by decreased foraging and increased retreat behaviour). These findings emphasize the impor­
tance of understanding how animals respond to different sensory components of stimuli to improve captive 
management practices, enhance welfare, and ultimately help the conservation of endangered species.
1. Introduction
Animals in zoos inhabit environments that, while designed for their 
care, are inherently artificial and shaped extensively by human activity. 
As a result, human-generated sights, sounds, and smells, ranging from 
visitor presence to construction or routine maintenance, become 
embedded in the sensory landscape that animals must navigate. 
Although numerous studies have investigated the effects of anthropo­
genic disturbance on free-living wildlife (Harding et al., 2019; Shannon 
et al., 2016), very few studies have studied how the specific sensory 
components of these anthropogenic activities, such as visual (e.g. human 
presence, movement, and novel objects) and acoustic (e.g. human voices 
and mechanical noise) components, differentially affect free-living and 
captive wildlife (Clark and Dunn, 2022; Halfwerk and Slabbekoorn, 
2015; Munoz and Blumstein, 2012). This leaves a significant gap in the 
research. The majority of studies have focused on the overall effect of 
human stimuli, typically characterized as the ‘visitor effect’ (Collins 
et al., 2017; Davey, 2007; Farrand, 2007; Fernandez et al., 2009; Hosey, 
2000; Sherwen and Hemsworth, 2019; Su´arez et al., 2017), rather than 
determining which components of human activity drive behavioural or 
welfare responses. Only by separating and testing an animal’s response 
in these ecologically relevant sensory components, both in isolation and

in combination, can we fully understand the ways in which they disturb 
them and make better evidence-based animal management decisions.
The current study used pied tamarins (Saguinus bicolor) as a model 
species that is threatened with extinction in the wild, acoustically sen­
sitive, and important to manage effectively in ex-situ populations (zoos). 
Anthropogenic activities pose a pervasive and ongoing challenge for 
pied tamarins, affecting them both in their natural habitats and in 
captivity (Gordo et al., 2021; Sobroza et al., 2024a, 2024b; Steinbrecher 
et al., 2023; Wormell et al., 1996). In the wild, pied tamarins inhabit 
forest patches that are increasingly fragmented, and rapidly being lost, 
in and around Manaus, Brazil, which is one of the fastest growing cities 
in the Amazon Basin (Gordo et al., 2013). Pied tamarins have the 
smallest range of any Amazonian primate (~7500 km2) (Gordo et al., 
2013), are classified as Critically Endangered by the IUCN (Gordo et al., 
2021), and are considered one of the world’s 25 most endangered pri­
mate species (Mittermeier et al., 2024). Captive pied tamarins often 
show highly activated physiological and behavioural stress responses 
(Price et al., 2019), and have chronic physical health problems (Cabana 
et al., 2018; Richards-Rios et al., 2021) and poor breeding success 
(Armstrong and Santymire, 2013; L´opez et al., 2001; Wormell et al., 
1996).
We examined pied tamarin behaviour and use of space in response to 
the visual and acoustic components of two anthropogenic activities: 1) 
close enclosure proximity of zoo visitors; and 2) construction activity. 
Based on the threat-sensitive predator avoidance hypothesis (Bishop and 
Brown, 1992; Lind and Cresswell, 2005; Monclús et al., 2009; Papworth 
et al., 2013), we expected pied tamarins to evaluate and respond to 
anthropogenic activity according to whether they perceived it as 
threatening or not. This would be shown in their behaviour and use of 
space. We predicted that if stimuli were perceived as threatening, pied 
tamarins would move away and position themselves higher in the 
enclosure and/or increase defensive or avoidance behaviours, but that 
no changes would be observable if stimuli were perceived as 
non-threatening. We also hypothesised that congruent conditions (i.e., 
two components from the same stimulus, e.g., acoustic and visual 
components of visitors) would provide more relevant information to 
tamarins, and therefore cause a different response than incongruent 
conditions or components in isolation. More specifically we predicted 
that when confronted with incongruent stimuli (i.e., two components 
from different stimuli, e.g., acoustic component of construction and vi­
sual components of visitors) or components in isolation, the information 
would be less reliable for the tamarins to assess a potential threat, and 
thus they would attempt to gather more information, and/or positioning 
themselves higher to increase visibility and safety.
2. Methods
2.1. Subjects and housing
Experiments were conducted on 12 pair-housed pied tamarins 
housed at Jersey Zoo, British Channel Islands, between May and August 
2022. Subjects were five males and seven females, ranging from seven to 
23 years of age. All subjects were captive bred and kept in non-breeding 
pairs; three of these were mixed-sex and three were same-sex (Table S1).
Pairs of tamarins were housed in similar enclosures in three different 
building locations across the zoo. Enclosures had indoor (mean 18 m3, 
SD = 8.19) and an outdoor (mean 68 m3, SD = 38.57) areas connected 
by a small (0.5 × 0.5 m) opening with a sliding door. Pied tamarins 
housed in the same building were separated by an empty enclosure 
between them, thus avoiding any direct contact. Subjects had access to 
their outdoor areas during the day (08:00–16:30 h), but were kept in­
doors overnight. The indoor areas were equipped with a nest box, 
feeding platform, and several wooden planks, ladders, and ropes. The 
outdoor areas had a variety of plants and foliage and were equipped with 
tree trunks, ropes, and natural branches. Subjects were fed a mix of 
commercial primate pellets in the morning (08:30–09:30 h), fruits and 
vegetables at noon (12:00–13:00 h), and live insects in the afternoon 
(16:00–17:00 h). Food was provided in bowls and live insects were 
scattered on their food platforms.
2.2. Experimental design
The experiment consisted of a 3 × 3 paradigm with nine conditions: a 
baseline (no stimuli); two visual stimuli (the visual component of visi­
tors or construction); two acoustic stimuli (the acoustic component of 
visitors or construction); and four combined visual and acoustic stimuli 
that were either congruent (e.g., the visual and acoustic components of 
visitors) or incongruent (e.g., the visual component of visitors and the 
acoustic component of construction) (Table 1).
2.3. Experimental procedure
Each tamarin pair was exposed to each of the nine experimental 
conditions (Table 1) once, yielding 54 trials. Each tamarin pair partici­
pated in one trial per day. The order of conditions for each pair was 
randomised without replacement using a random number generator, 
although if one of the pairs was exposed to sound playback, the other 
pair at the same location was not exposed to sound playback on the same 
day (i.e., pairs 2, 3 and 4 or pairs 5 and 6; Table S1). Trials took place 
between 13:00 and 16:00 h to avoid coinciding with routine husbandry, 
and each trial was 5-min duration with approximately 3-min setup and 
3-min take-down time either side. For all conditions including baseline, 
the speaker was installed within 3-min of arrival and the observer 
moved in front of the enclosure to allow enough time for the subjects to 
settle and behave naturally. A trial was only started when both tamarins 
were in their outside enclosure. For conditions with a visual and/or 
acoustic component, the visual component was put in place, and/or 
acoustic playback started, immediately before the start of the 5-min 
trial. At the end of the 5-min trial, all stimuli were removed, and the 
researchers left within 3-min.
2.4. Visual stimuli
Visitor visual stimulus: Three adult zoo volunteers behaved as a 
cohesive group. They were dressed in ordinary, day-to-day clothes (not 
resembling zoo staff, who wear uniforms). All volunteers were unfa­
miliar to the subjects. Although the volunteers were not real zoo visitors, 
their unfamiliarity and lack of prior contact with the subjects, as well as 
their visitor-like behaviour and appearance, ensured they were likely to 
be perceived similarly, allowing us to treat them as “visitors” in this 
context of this experiment. During visitor visual conditions, the group 
walked slowly towards the outside enclosure until they were 1 m in front 
of it, then stood in silence. Construction visual stimulus: A mock con­
struction site was installed at the same distance as the visitor visual 
stimulus, 1 m in front of the enclosure. This consisted of two red and 
white plastic traffic cones (~71 cm height) spaced 1.5 m apart with two 
rows of chains hanging between. A bright yellow and black construction 
sign was placed next to one of the cones (~50 cm height). This combi­
nation of items meant that installation was quick at the start of a trial. In 
addition, the construction mock-up site was intentionally designed 
without human workers, featuring only stationary objects typically 
found at construction sites, to make a clear distinction between the vi­
sual components of the two stimuli.
2.5. Acoustic stimuli
Visitor acoustic stimulus: The sounds of natural human conversation 
including spontaneous laughter and brief moments of silence was 
recorded from five adult zoo volunteers and edited to match the acoustic 
characteristics of typical visitor noise. Construction acoustic stimulus: 
The sounds of hammering, drilling, metal cutting and ‘clanging’ of metal 
pieces was recorded from real construction work taking place

approximately 150 m from the closest study enclosures. Recordings 
were made at a 1–2 m horizontal distance away from the epicenter of the 
source at a sampling rate of 44.1 kHz with a 16-bit resolution, using a 
linear PCM Tascam DR40 recorder (TEAC Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) 
and a handheld Shotgun Microphone (Røde, Silverwater, Australia) with 
a windshield. The sound files were then edited with Audacity software 
(Audacity® version 3.0.2) to produce s 5-min file (.wav file) for each 
stimulus, which included a 30-sec fading-in period at the beginning of 
the recording and a 30-sec fading-out period at the end to avoid startling 
the animals with an abrupt change (Wale et al., 2013).
Playback files were played through a speaker (Chiayo Focus Pro 
U0PC) positioned on the ground 1.5 m from the outdoor enclosure and 
oriented towards the center of it. The speaker was camouflaged with 
vegetation and playback was started remotely via Bluetooth. To control 
for the possibility of the speaker also being a visual stimulus, it was also 
placed in the same location (but switched off) for all other conditions 
including baseline. The sound pressure levels of playbacks were based 
on the sound pressure levels (measured with an SPL meter (Precision 
Gold NO5CC Sound Level Meter, Maplin Electronics Ltd, Rotherham, 
UK)) at which they ‘naturally’ occurred when the recordings were made 
(visitor sound: peak amplitude 65 dB SPL at 1.5 m; construction sound: 
peak amplitude 75 dB SPL at a 1.5 m distance).
2.6. Data collection
Observations of animals were only made when subjects were present 
in their outdoor enclosures. Behavioural data were collected using a 
combination of instantaneous sampling and one-zero sampling, as 
different behaviours measures required different recording approaches 
(Altmann, 1974). All behaviours were recorded referring to an ethogram 
(Table 2).
Instantaneous sampling was used to record state variables that could 
be reliably assessed at a single moment in time (Altmann, 1974), 
including the individual’s general activity state and their use of space 
within the enclosure. The subject’s use of space in the enclosure was 
recorded in height and depth categories. For this, the enclosure was 
visually divided into three equal height levels, low (including the 
ground), middle, and high; and three equal depth levels, front (including 
the enclosure fence) middle, and back (Figure S1).
One–zero sampling was used to record the occurrence of discrete or 
infrequent event behaviours (i.e., looking, chatting, scratching, and 
scent marking), where the primary interest was whether a behaviour 
occurred within a defined time window rather than its frequency 
(Bassett et al., 2003; Blumstein et al., 2006).
Sampling was conducted at 15-second intervals. Within each pair, 
observations alternated between individuals, resulting in a 30-second 
sampling interval per individual and a total of 10 sampling points per 
individual per 5-min trial. This alternating approach was necessary due 
to dense foliage within the enclosures, which limited continuous 
simultaneous visibility of both subjects and therefore allowed for more 
accurate and focused recording of each individual’s behaviour.
2.7. Statistical analyses
All analyses were performed using R version 3.5.2 (R Core Team, 
2022). A Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was conducted to reduce 
the number of observed behaviours into fewer variables and therefore 
minimise model complexity. As several behaviours were assumed to be 
correlated, PCA was carried out with an oblique rotation (i.e., Promax

rotation), which allows for components to be correlate. This approach 
allowed us to consider and identify these correlations, reduce the 
number of variables, simplify models for easier interpretability, and 
reduce statistical errors, particular for an experiment with relatively 
small sample size (Budaev, 2010; Costello and Osborne, 2019). PCA 
analysis included all frequently observed behaviours (forage, vigilant, 
move, rest, retreat, look, chatter and scratch) from all trials and was 
done using the ‘prcomp’ function from the ‘FactoMineR’ package (Lˆe 
et al., 2008) as well as the ‘psych::principal’ function from the ‘psych’ 
package (Revelle, 2023). Other behaviours were excluded from the 
analysis, due to their limited number of occurrences (less than five oc­
currences). Principal Components (PCs) were included in further ana­
lyses based on inspection of the Kaiser criterion (inclusion of 
components with an eigenvalue greater than 1), the empirical Bayesian 
Information Criterion (BIC; models with the lowest BIC are preferred), 
the Very Simple Structure (VSS; evaluation of the best fitting individual 
components/factors) and Standardized Root Mean Square Residuals (or 
SRMR; average difference between the implied and the observed cor­
relation) (Morton and Altschul, 2019).
The extracted PCs were analysed with generalised linear mixed- 
effects models (GLMM) using the lme4 (Bates et al., 2015) and 
glmmTMB packages (Brooks et al., 2017). Each PC was a dependent 
variable analysed in an individual model. To investigate the different 
effect of each condition on the PCs, each component (visual and 
acoustic) was classified by its three possible presentations (no stimulus, 
visitor stimulus, and construction stimulus) and used as an independent 
variable with three levels. To account for repeated observations, subject 
ID was included as a random factor in the model. Model assumptions 
were tested using the DHARMa package (Hartig, 2022), which provides 
simulation-based 
residual 
diagnostics 
to 
test 
for 
dispersion, 
zero-inflation, and uniformity. In addition, we visually examined 
QQ-plots to confirm adequate model fit. Assumptions were satisfied for 
PC1 and PC2, but not for PC3. Therefore, a log transformation was 
applied to the PC3 model, which resolved the violation of assumptions. 
The subjects’ use of space was analysed following the same model 
structure and analysis.
2.8. Ethical note
This study was ethically approved by Durrell Wildlife Conservation 
Trust’s Ethics Committee (#ETH19/1112, 21 January 2019) and Anglia 
Ruskin University (FREP/SREP number: A&EB DREP19–126, 09/12/ 
19). All subjects were housed under their normal management condi­
tions and were not deprived of food or water. Playbacks consisted of 
sounds that already occurred around the zoo and thus were not entirely 
novel to the animals. In addition, playbacks were played at noise 
amplitude levels that did not exceed normal levels of each stimulus.
Human speech recordings consisted of five zoo volunteers having a 
conversation. Prior to recordings, the participants were fully informed 
about the use of the recordings in the experiment and the experiment 
itself. All participants gave verbal consent and no personal or sensitive 
information was discussed nor recorded. In addition, recordings were 
deleted after the conclusion of the experiment.
3. Results
3.1. Principal component analysis (PCA)
Three Principal Components (PCs) were included for further analysis 
based on the criteria described in the methods. The first three compo­
nents explained a combined 72.2 % of the total variance (PC1 = 35.5 %; 
PC2 = 19.9 %; and PC3 = 16.8 %).
PC1 had positive loadings for vigilance, looking, and chattering 
(Table 3). PC1 was thus characterised as ‘vigilance’. In contrast, PC2 had 
positive loadings for foraging and negative loadings for retreating. We 
therefore labelled PC2 as ‘environmental engagement’. PC3 had positive 
loadings for resting. Thus, PC3 was considered a general measure of 
‘rest’ (Table 3).
3.2. Behavioural analysis
Compared to baseline, the behaviour of pied tamarins was found to 
be significantly affected by experimental condition.
PC1 (‘vigilance’) was significantly impacted by visual components 
(GLMM: F2,88 = 30.382, P < 0.001), and the interaction between visual 
and acoustic components (GLMM: F4,88 = 3.075, P = 0.020). These 
findings were driven by the significant positive impact of the visual 
component of visitors (GLMM: t88 = 5.605, P < 0.001) and the acoustic 
component of visitors (GLMM: t88 = 2.269, P 0.026), and the significant 
negative interaction between the visual and acoustic components of 
visitors (GLMM: t88 = −2.925, P = 0.005). Given that vigilance, looking 
and chattering were all positively loaded onto PC1, significant positive 
effects meant an increase in these behaviours, whereas significant 
negative effects meant a decrease. No other condition had a significant 
effect on PC1 (Fig. 1a; Fig. 2a).
PC2 (‘environmental engagement’) was significantly affected by the 
acoustic components (ANOVA: F2,88 = 4.307, P = 0.016). This finding 
was driven by the significant negative effect of the acoustic components 
of construction on PC2 (GLMM: t88 = 2.988, P = 0.041). As foraging was 
positively loaded, and retreating negatively loaded onto PC2, significant 
negative effects on PC2 meant a decrease in foraging and an increase in 
retreating behaviour. No other condition had a significant impact on 
PC2 (Fig. 1b; Fig. 2b).
PC3 (‘rest’) was not predicted by experimental condition specifically 
or overall (Fig. 1c; Fig. 2c).
3.3. Use of space analysis
Use of space in the enclosure was analysed by both depth (front, 
middle, back) and height (low, middle, high). Depth in the enclosure 
was significantly influenced by visual components (GLMM: F2754 =
23.475, P < 0.001), acoustic components (GLMM: F2754 = 7.105, 
P = 0.001), and the interaction between them (GLMM: F2755 = 4.236, 
P = 0.002). This was largely driven by the strong positive impact (i.e., 
increase in depth) of the construction visual (GLMM: t754 = 2.205, 
P = 0.028) and the construction acoustic components (GLMM: t754=
4.317, P < 0.001) as well as the interaction between the visual and 
acoustic components of construction (GLMM: t755= −3.360, P = 0.001) 
and the visual component of visitor and the acoustic component of 
construction (GLMM: t755= −2.989, P = 0.003) (Fig. 3a; Fig. 4a).
Height in the enclosure was significantly influenced by the visual 
components (GLMM: F2754 = 6.746, P = 0.001), the acoustic compo­
nents (GLMM: F2754 = 15.873, P < 0.001) and the interaction between 
them (GLMM: F2755 = 4.958, P = 0.001). This was largely driven by the 
strong positive impact (i.e., increase in height) of the visual component 
of visitors (GLMM: t754 = 3.297, P = 0.001), the visual component of

4. Discussion
This study examined the behavioural responses of pied tamarins, a 
critically endangered primate species, to the visual and acoustic com­
ponents of zoo visitors and construction activity. Our results revealed 
four key findings: (1) different anthropogenic stimuli (visitors, con­
struction) and the components comprising those anthropogenic stimuli 
(visual, acoustic), caused significant differences in behavioural re­
sponses and use of space; (2) isolated components (only visual or 
acoustic) generally elicited stronger behavioural responses than com­
binations of components; (3) incongruent combinations of components 
did not lead to any significant changes in behaviour, while congruous 
combinations of components for visitors showed a significantly lower 
response than isolated stimuli; (4) both visual and acoustic components 
influenced the use of space, although construction sound had a partic­
ularly pronounced effect on the depth that tamarins went to in the 
enclosure, while both types of anthropogenic stimuli (visitor, con­
struction) had significant impacts on height.
4.1. Responses to visitor stimuli
Pied tamarins, belonging to the family Callitrichidae (tamarins, lion 
tamarins, and marmosets), are among the smallest primate species, with 
an adult weight of around 500 g (Gordo et al., 2013). They face pre­
dation from a wide range of animals (Hart, 2007) and have developed 
diverse antipredation strategies aimed at early detection of potential 
threats (Ferrari, 2009; Ferrari and Ferrari, 1990). Therefore, when 
confronted with visitors, an increase in vigilance, as seen in our results, 
was expected. Looking more closely at visitors as a visual stimulus may 
explain why they elicit a strong response from pied tamarins. Visitors 
approaching the enclosure from different angles, often using unpre­
dictable and sudden movements, gestures, and facial expressions, could 
potentially convey dominance, aggression, or a potential threat. For 
example, eye contact or teeth baring are well characterized behavioural 
indicators of aggression and dominance in nonhuman primates (Kim 
et al., 2022; Petersen et al., 2018), and thus visitors looking at or smiling 
could have been perceived as threatening, frightening, or stressful 
(Chiew et al., 2019; Hosey, 2000, 2013). The use of space analysis 
corroborated the strong significant effect of visitors on the ‘vigilance’ 
response when tamarins were confronted with visitors visually. 
Although statistically insignificant, pied tamarins chose to be closer to 
the front of the enclosure in the presence of visitors, compared to con­
ditions with no visitor presence or the construction visual condition. 
Similar results were shown by Wormell et al. (1996), who also found 
that zoo-housed pied tamarins responded to visitors with approach and 
threat (piloerection and chatter) more often than other callitrichid 
species.
Moreover, the unfamiliarity of the visitors could have reinforced 
increased vigilance responses. Although all of our study subjects lived in 
a zoo and were familiar with the presence of visitors, most of them were 
housed in off-show enclosures inaccessible to visitors. This suggested 
that while they might have been housed in on-show enclosures at some 
point and thus had experienced visitors before, visitors were still a 
novelty. Past research has shown that zoo-housed primates can differ­
entiate between different categories of human, such as keepers, staff and 
unfamiliar visitors (Smith, 2014, 2016) and therefore familiarity (i.e., 
habituation) played an important role. For practicality reasons, the in­
clusion of more on-show individuals in our study sample was not 
possible and therefore, an extension or repetition of the study, including 
on-show individuals, might provide more insight into this possibility. 
However, for tamarins that are on-show, visitor noise and visitor 
behaviour near a test group would be harder to control.
4.2. Responses to construction stimuli
Construction sound in isolation caused a decrease in environmental 
engagement (PC2). In addition, construction sound in isolation was 
associated with changes in enclosure space use. Pied tamarins either 
retreated to the indoor enclosure or preferred to stay at the back of the 
outdoor enclosure when confronted with the acoustic component alone 
(depth). One possible explanation may be the design of the construction 
stimulus. For this experiment, the construction mock-up site was 
intentionally designed not to include people and only feature stationary 
objects typically found at construction sites, to make a clear distinction 
from the visual components of the visitor stimulus. This possibly made 
the construction stimulus a rather static and perceptually dull event 
against the normal zoo background. In contrast, the acoustic construc­
tion stimulus could have been a perceptually strong event, with the peak 
amplitude of the construction playback equivalent to the lower end of 
construction noise levels of 75 dB (Neitzel et al., 1999). This was in fact 
10 dB higher than the level produced by visitors, and therefore twice as 
loud. Furthermore, construction noise was characterised by sporadic, 
sudden, and sharp noises, such as hammering, clanging, and drilling.

Like the sudden movements of visitors, these noises could potentially 
startle, stress and/or introduce uncertainty (Hemsworth, 2003; Rushen 
et al., 1999) and therefore cause a behavioural response in the animals, 
in this case a decrease in environmental engagement (defined by 
reduced foraging and increased retreat behaviour). While construction 
sites are often seen as dynamic and visually engaging due to the presence 
of workers and ongoing activities, it is also worth noting that in zoo 
facilities, or other captive environments, mitigation measures are often 
put in place to shield animals from the disturbance of construction 
events. These measures typically involve stationary barriers (hoarding 
screens) that obscure the direct view of the construction site from the 
animals. Therefore, these barriers represent the main visual cue 
perceived by animals and the argument that construction sites are 
inherently dynamic and visually stimulating overlooks the reality for 
animals in captive environments.
While specific research on the impact of construction events is 
limited, spatial avoidance appears to be a common coping mechanism 
for a variety of animals exposed to such stimuli (Chosy et al., 2014; 
Jakob-Hoff et al., 2019). For instance, a study at an urban zoo in New 
Zealand showed that some of the study species i.e., giraffes (Giraffa 
camelopardalis), elephants (Elephas maximus), and emus (Dromaius 
novaehollandiae), moved to quieter areas when exposed to continuous or 
intermittent construction noise (Jakob-Hoff et al., 2019). Studies on 
callitrichids (common marmoset, Callithrix jacchus; cotton-top tamarin, 
Saguinus oedipus; and pied tamarins) have also shown that when given 
the choice between quiet and noisy areas (featuring music, waterfalls, 
and white noise), the quiet areas are preferred (McDermott and Hauser, 
2007; Wark et al., 2023). In addition, the use of space analysis demon­
strated that height played a significant role when tamarins were con­
fronted with either visual or acoustic components individually. Visual 
and acoustic components for both visitors and construction stimuli 
caused pied tamarins to retreat to greater heights. This is in line with 
past research that showed that there is a vertical axis of fear in arboreal 
primates, meaning that they feel safer with increasing height (Emerson 
et al., 2011; Nowak et al., 2014). Given that pied tamarins are a small 
arboreal primate species, it is unsurprising that they seek higher ground 
when faced with uncertainty.
4.3. Combined stimuli in comparison to stimuli in isolation
Although some components might dominate over others in specific 
contexts, the combination of information received via multiple sensory 
modalities can provide a more comprehensive understanding of the 
environment, potentially improving decision-making (Narins et al., 
2005; Partan et al., 2010; Partan and Marler, 2005). Multisensory 
integration, i.e., the combined use of the senses, appears to be adaptive 
for behavioural tasks such as foraging, orientation, mate choice, or 
predator/prey interactions (Higham and Hebets, 2013; Partan et al., 
2010). Based on this, we hypothesized those congruous combinations of 
components would enable pied tamarins to evaluate and understand 
stimuli more effectively than incongruous stimuli, allowing them to 
determine whether or not it posed a threat and respond accordingly.
Our results support this hypothesis in the case of visitor stimuli, 
where congruent combinations, i.e., visitor visual and acoustic stimuli in 
combination, significantly reduced the vigilance behavioural response 
of pied tamarins (PC1), compared to components in isolation. One 
possible explanation for a diminished response may lie in the context 
presentation. The visual component alone may lack sufficient context, 
while the addition of acoustic information creates a fuller sensory pic­
ture, enabling tamarins to assess the situation more effectively (Hebets 
and Papaj, 2005; Smith and Belk, 2001). This aligns with findings from 
studies on other species. For example, Partan et al. (2010) demonstrated 
that grey squirrels (Sciurus carolinensis) responded more strongly to 
combined alarm signals of a biomimetic squirrel (visual tail flags and 
auditory alarm barks) than to the presentation of either component in 
isolation, highlighting the additive value of congruent sensory 
information. Similarly, Hazlett and McLay (2005) found that intertidal 
crabs (Heterozius rotundifrons) reduced their freeze response when 
exposed to combined visual and olfactory cues. Although Hazlett and 
McLay (2005) suggested that the crabs might switch to a different 
anti-predatory strategy of finding shelter, the study still concluded that 
more information is conveyed, and more context is provided when 
multiple cues were presented through different sensory components.
However, our results reveal a different pattern for construction 
stimuli, where congruent conditions did not significantly reduce 
behavioural responses. This difference may be explained by examining 
the behavioural response to each of these individual components. For 
the visitor stimulus, both the visual and acoustic components signifi­
cantly increased vigilance behaviour independently, suggesting that 
their combination offered consistent and complementary information, 
enabling tamarins to evaluate the stimulus and moderate their response 
more effectively. In this case, the combination of both components led to 
a significant reduction in vigilance behaviour. In contrast, for con­
struction stimuli, only the acoustic component elicited an ‘environ­
mental engagement’ response (PC2), while the visual component did not 
lead to any clear behavioural change. This suggests that the visual and 
acoustic elements of construction stimuli are perceived differently, 
resulting in nonadditive effects when combined, and instead influence 
behaviour in different ways. These findings emphasise the importance of 
considering separate components of stimuli to fully understand animals’ 
behavioural responses. By investigating how animals perceive and 
respond to isolated and combined components, we can develop more 
targeted approaches to mitigate and address the impacts of anthropo­
genic disturbances, both in captivity and in the wild.
4.4. Future research directions
Our results show that behavioural responses in zoo-housed pied 
tamarins differ when visual and auditory components of stimuli are 
presented in isolation compared to when they are combined. These re­
sults have important husbandry, management, and welfare implications 
for pied tamarins, other callitrichids, or potentially other small arboreal 
mammals (Anderson, 2014; Bartlett et al., 2023; Browning and Maple, 
2019; Hebert and Bard, 2000; Ross and Lukas, 2006).
4.4.1. Reducing the impact of visitors
Since visitors have been shown to induce strong behavioural changes 
compared to baseline, especially increased vigilance and proximity to 
visitors, measures that limit close contact with visitors is recommended 
to ensure the welfare of these sensitive species, (Sherwen and Hems­
worth, 2019; Wormell et al.,1996). More importantly, our results 
showed that the congruent combination of visual and auditory visitor 
stimuli reduced behavioural responses across all three PCs, highlighting 
the importance of allowing pied tamarins access to both sensory mo­
dalities to evaluate potential threats.
Given these findings, mitigation measures such as full visual barriers 
or relocating animals to off-show enclosures should be carefully 
considered. While these strategies may reduce distress, they could 
inadvertently limit tamarins’ ability to assess and respond appropriately 
to their environment. Instead of removing the visual aspect of visitors for 
the animal, allowing the animal to choose and control their visibility of 
visitors, could offer a good alternative. For example, enclosures with 
multiple areas, e.g., an outdoor compartment in addition to an indoor 
compartment, or those that offer partial visibility, e.g., dense vegetation 
or camouflage netting, allow animals to retreat or engage with their 
surroundings. Clark et al. (2012) found that privacy screens inside the 
enclosure and on external windows allowed zoo-housed Western low­
land gorillas (Gorilla gorilla gorilla) to have more control over visual 
access to visitors, which reduced their levels of vigilance. Several studies 
have found that offering choice within enclosures reduces stereotypical 
or undesired behaviours (Badihi, 2006; Ross, 2006), promotes social 
behaviours (Kurtycz et al., 2014; Rust et al., 2024), and decreases

physiological stress (Kaplan et al., 2012; Owen et al., 2004), thus posi­
tively affecting welfare.
4.4.2. Reducing the impact of construction
Traditional mitigation measures often involve stationary barriers 
that buffer the sounds but also obscure the direct view of the construc­
tion site from the animals. Although sound barriers can effectively 
dampen noise levels (Orban et al., 2017), the cost of not being able to see 
the source of noise could potentially outweigh the benefits of reduced 
noise intensity. Future research should investigate the use of alternative 
barriers and evaluate the trade-off between visual and sound penetra­
tion of such barriers. For example, dense vegetation, such as trees, 
shrubs, and ground cover, may provide a good alternative. Natural 
barriers provide partial visibility while also effectively reducing noise 
levels (Fan et al., 2019; Ozdemir et al., 2014; Yofianti and Usman, 
2021). Furthermore, natural barriers are more naturalistic and visually 
pleasing compared to the usual metal barriers, and thus also enhance the 
visual aesthetics of the zoo environment (Hong and Jeon, 2014).
In addition, although retreat were the primary responses when pied 
tamarins were confronted with construction noise (PC2), we recom­
mend not confining the animals to their inside enclosures when such 
activity is underway. It is important to allow animals choice by giving 
them permanent access to all parts of the enclosure, especially during 
construction or similar loud events. A highly enriched and foliage-dense 
outdoor enclosure design further can also help encourage outside ac­
tivity to further reduce stress (Kaplan et al., 2012; Sha et al., 2016).
4.4.3. Limitations and implications for captive and wild populations
While this study is one of the first studies attempting to experimen­
tally disentangle visual and acoustic components of different stimuli, it 
is important to recognise some methodological limitations. Most of these 
limitations arose from practical considerations of the experimental 
design. First, visitors were represented by volunteers and thus subtle 
differences from genuine visitor interactions cannot be ruled out. 
However, their unfamiliarity and lack of prior contact with the subjects, 
as well as their visitor-like behaviour and appearance ensured they were 
likely to be perceived similarly, allowing us to treat them as “visitors” in 
this context of this experiment. Similarly, the audio recordings were 
based on simple conversations among volunteers and therefore may not 
fully capture the variability of real zoo soundscapes. This approach was 
adopted due to ethical and data protection reasons, as it ensured full 
consent for recordings and avoided capturing confidential or sensitive 
information. However, the recordings included natural conservation 
features, such as spontaneous laughter and brief pauses and were edited 
to match the acoustic characteristics of typical visitor noise. The con­
struction visual stimulus was also simplified, consisting of two red and 
white plastic cones and a yellow sign. While a larger installation 
incorporating construction equipment or machinery (e.g. scaffolding, 
cement mixer, or power tools), practical constraints limited this choice. 
In addition, because pied tamarins are a critically endangered and 
highly sensitive species, all stimuli also had to strike a careful balance 
between novelty, familiarity and intensity to evoke measurable re­
actions without causing undue stress. Future studies would benefit from 
extending this approach to larger sample sizes, across multiple in­
stitutions, and species. This would help strengthen the robustness of the 
results, as well as clarify the broader relevance of multimodal anthro­
pogenic stimuli for animal behaviour. Despite these methodology con­
straints, this study provided valuable insight into the behavioural 
responses of captive pied tamarins to isolated and combined visual and 
acoustic components of anthropogenic stimuli and highlights the 
importance of accounting for the multimodal nature of stimuli, as well as 
offering important recommendations for future research.
While captive studies allow for controlled observations, their find­
ings may not fully translate to wild populations. Captive animals, living 
within the anthropogenic environment and being constrained behind 
physical barriers, are less able to evade anthropogenic impacts. 
Although anthropogenic stimuli, including humans themselves, are 
stimuli that animals have had little evolutionary time to adapt to, their 
‘natural’ behaviour might already be influenced and/or modified by 
anthropogenic conditions, leading to potentially different responses 
from those of their wild counterparts. It should also be noted that 
although pied tamarins in the wild have a better capacity to avoid 
anthropogenic stimuli, their home range is extremely restricted, making 
them highly impacted by human pressure. Therefore, further studies are 
needed to investigate whether effects similar to those we observed also 
occur in the wild.
5. Conclusion
This study emphasised the importance of considering the sensory 
components of stimuli both individually and in combination. Overall, 
our results show the diverse responses exhibited by pied tamarins when 
exposed to different stimuli, with specific components playing a pivotal 
role in shaping their reactions. To fully understand the ecological impact 
of human activity and these complex anthropogenic disturbances on 
animal welfare, future research should account for the fact that stimuli 
often contain multiple sensory components, which can independently 
drive behavioural responses. The findings also highlight the importance 
of considering appropriate mitigation measures to support captive 
husbandry needs, welfare, and ultimately the conservation of the pied 
tamarin.
